It is also home to mugger crocodiles, migratory birds, monitor lizards, and a surprisingly rich ecosystem — all within a 20-minute drive from Bandra Kurla Complex. The lakeside promenade, the IIT Bombay campus, and the restaurant-lined Hiranandani Gardens nearby make Powai one of Mumbai's most pleasant half-day destinations.
history:
Powai Lake was constructed between 1889 and 1891 as part of the Bombay Improvement Trust's water supply infrastructure. The British administration, facing chronic water shortages in a city whose population was growing rapidly, commissioned a series of reservoirs in the hills east of Bombay. Powai was the largest, designed to collect monsoon runoff from the Powai and Vihar catchment areas.

The lake served as Mumbai's primary water source until the larger Vihar and Tulsi lakes took over that role in the 20th century. Released from purely utilitarian function, Powai became a leisure destination. The establishment of the IIT Bombay campus on its northern shore in 1958 brought academic life to the area and helped preserve the surrounding ecology from the development pressure that consumed most of suburban Mumbai.

The mugger crocodiles (Crocodylus palustris) in Powai Lake are the lake's most dramatic residents — a breeding population that has lived here for decades. Their presence has made the lake a protected zone and prevented the kind of waterfront development that might otherwise have occurred. Sightings from the lakeside promenade, particularly early in the morning, are not uncommon.

timeline.event: Powai Lake constructed as a freshwater reservoir for Bombay
IIT Bombay established on the lake's northern shore
Hiranandani Gardens township developed on the lake's southern edge
Lake ecosystem documented; crocodile population protected under wildlife laws

address: Powai Lake, Powai, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400076

entryFee.note: Free. Do not enter the water — crocodiles are present. IIT Bombay campus requires entry permission on weekends.

openingHours.note: Lakeside promenade open during daylight hours

name: Red Fort (Lal Qila)
tagline: The seat of Mughal power for 200 years — and the stage where India declared independence on August 15, 1947.
description: The Red Fort is the most politically significant monument in India. Built by Emperor Shah Jahan between 1638 and 1648 as the centrepiece of his new capital Shahjahanabad — the seventh city of Delhi — it served as the primary residence of the Mughal emperors for over two centuries, until the last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar was tried for treason here after the 1857 uprising and exiled to Rangoon. But the Fort's most resonant moment came on August 15, 1947, when Jawaharlal Nehru raised the Indian tricolour from its ramparts for the first time and delivered the Tryst with Destiny speech — a tradition that every Indian Prime Minister has continued every Independence Day since. The fort derives its name from the massive red sandstone ramparts that enclose it — walls 33 metres high at their tallest, extending for 2.4 kilometres around the complex. Inside is a world: the Diwan-i-Aam (Hall of Public Audience), where the emperor received his subjects; the Diwan-i-Khas (Hall of Private Audience), which housed the legendary Peacock Throne until Nadir Shah carried it to Persia in 1739; the Khas Mahal (the emperor's private chambers); the Rang Mahal (Palace of Colours), where the emperor's wives and concubines lived; the Moti Masjid (Pearl Mosque), a perfectly proportioned white marble mosque built by Aurangzeb; and the Mumtaz Mahal, now housing the Archaeological Museum. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2007.
history:
Shah Jahan, who had already built the Taj Mahal at Agra as a mausoleum for his wife Mumtaz Mahal, decided in 1638 to move his capital from Agra to Delhi and build a new city — Shahjahanabad — with a new fort at its heart. The Red Fort took approximately ten years to construct, employing a workforce of thousands under the supervision of the chief architect Ustad Ahmad Lahori (who also designed the Taj Mahal). It was completed and occupied in 1648.

The fort's design is a masterwork of Mughal military and palace architecture. The ramparts, built from the same red Agra sandstone used for the Taj Mahal's base platform, are both defensive and ceremonial — they announce imperial power before a single room is seen. The Lahori Gate on the western side and the Delhi Gate on the southern are the main entrances; the Lahori Gate faces the commercial street of Chandni Chowk, which Shah Jahan also planned and which remains one of Old Delhi's primary arteries.

The interior complex was designed as a self-contained imperial world. At its peak under Shah Jahan and his successors Aurangzeb and Farrukhsiyar, the fort housed thousands of residents — the imperial family, concubines, servants, soldiers, artisans, and merchants. The Yamuna River ran directly alongside the eastern wall, providing both water and a natural defensive barrier.

The fort's history darkens after 1739, when the Persian emperor Nadir Shah invaded India, sacked Delhi, and massacred an estimated 30,000 inhabitants in a single day — the Qatl-e-Aam. He took back to Persia the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond. The Mughal empire never recovered its prestige, and the fort began a slow decline from imperial showpiece to administrative base. After the 1857 uprising — the Sepoy Mutiny — the British tried Emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar in the Diwan-i-Khas, exiled him, and converted much of the fort into British military barracks, demolishing many of the garden structures and residential buildings in the process.

India's independence was formally announced from the fort on August 15, 1947, when Nehru raised the flag from the ramparts. Every Independence Day since, the Prime Minister addresses the nation from the same spot — making the Red Fort the symbolic beating heart of Indian democracy.

timeline.event: Emperor Shah Jahan begins construction of the Red Fort as the centrepiece of his new capital Shahjahanabad
Fort completed; Shah Jahan moves his court from Agra to Delhi
Nadir Shah sacks Delhi; Peacock Throne and Koh-i-Noor diamond removed to Persia
The Uprising — Bahadur Shah Zafar, last Mughal emperor, tried in the Diwan-i-Khas and exiled to Rangoon
Jawaharlal Nehru raises India's flag from the ramparts on August 15; delivers the Tryst with Destiny speech
UNESCO designates Red Fort Complex a World Heritage Site

address: Netaji Subhash Marg, Lal Qila, Chandni Chowk, New Delhi, Delhi 110006

entryFee.note: Sound and Light Show ticketed separately (₹80 Indians / ₹150 foreigners). Archaeological Museum included in entry. Closed Mondays.

openingHours.note: Last entry 30 minutes before closing
Closed

name: Humayun's Tomb
tagline: The prototype of the Taj Mahal — built 93 years earlier, and still the most perfectly composed building in Delhi.
description: Humayun's Tomb is the most important Mughal monument in Delhi and one of the most architecturally significant buildings in all of South Asia. Built in 1570 by Emperor Humayun's senior widow Bega Begum, it was the first garden-tomb in the Indian subcontinent — a concept that would reach its ultimate expression 93 years later with the Taj Mahal, whose architect was almost certainly inspired directly by this structure. The building sits at the centre of a vast formal Mughal garden — the charbagh, the four-quartered Persian garden divided by water channels — and the geometry of approaching it through the garden across successive platforms of carved sandstone and marble is a carefully constructed experience of increasing grandeur. The tomb itself combines red sandstone with white marble in a double-domed structure that rises 47 metres — the first time a double dome was used in Indian architecture. More than 150 members of the Mughal imperial family are buried in the complex, earning it the nickname 'Dormitory of the Mughals.' UNESCO has designated it a World Heritage Site since 1993. The restoration, undertaken by the Aga Khan Trust for Culture in partnership with ASI over 20 years, is one of the most comprehensive heritage conservation projects undertaken in India and has returned the garden and building to a condition that approaches Shah Jahan-era documentation.
history:
Humayun, the second Mughal emperor and son of Babur, had a turbulent reign. He was twice defeated and exiled — once by the Afghan Sher Shah Suri, during which he spent 15 years in exile in Persia — before regaining his throne in 1555. He died in 1556 after falling down the stairs of his library in the Purana Qila. His senior wife Haji Begum (also called Bega Begum) commissioned this tomb in his honour in 1565, nearly a decade after his death.

The architect chosen was Mirak Mirza Ghiyas, a Persian architect who had worked at the Safavid court — his Persian training is evident in the building's composition, which draws directly from the mausoleum tradition of Timurid Iran and Central Asia. The garden was laid out in the formal Persian charbagh style, divided into four quadrants by water channels that represented the four rivers of paradise described in the Quran.

The building's proportions are considered near-perfect by architects and architectural historians. The platform alone is 7 metres high; the main structure rises 42 metres above it. The double dome — an outer dome and an inner dome with a space between them that allows the outer dome to be much taller without increasing the interior ceiling height — was used here for the first time in India and would become the defining element of Mughal architecture thereafter.

In 1857, the last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar took refuge in Humayun's Tomb during the Uprising against the British. He was captured here by British officer Major William Hodson, who executed the last Mughal princes at the Khooni Darwaza (Bloody Gate) nearby — one of the final chapters of the Mughal dynasty.

The Aga Khan Trust for Culture began its restoration in 1997 and has restored more than 30 structures in the complex. The charbagh garden has been replanted to match Mughal-era documentation. The work is internationally recognised as a model for large-scale heritage conservation.

timeline.event: Emperor Humayun dies after falling from his library stairs at the Purana Qila
Bega Begum commissions Humayun's tomb; construction begins under architect Mirak Mirza Ghiyas
Tomb completed; first garden-tomb in the Indian subcontinent
Last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar captured here by British forces after the Uprising
UNESCO designates Humayun's Tomb a World Heritage Site

address: Mathura Road, Hazrat Nizamuddin, New Delhi, Delhi 110013

entryFee.note: Children under 15 free. Complex also includes Isa Khan's Tomb, Bu Halima's Garden, and the Barber's Tomb — all included in the single ticket. Closed Mondays.

openingHours.note: Sunset visits particularly recommended
Closed

name: Qutb Minar
tagline: The tallest brick minaret in the world — and the oldest surviving Islamic monument in India.
description: The Qutb Minar is the most extraordinary structural achievement of medieval India and the oldest surviving monument of the Delhi Sultanate. Rising 72.5 metres from the rubble of what was once the Lal Kot fortress of the Rajput Tomars — the first of Delhi's seven historical cities — the minaret was begun by Qutb ud-Din Aibak in 1193, continued by his son-in-law Iltutmish, and completed in its present form by Firoz Shah Tughlaq in 1368, with each phase of construction visible in the changing stonework. The Qutb complex surrounding the minaret is as rich as the minaret itself: the Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque (literally 'The Might of Islam'), the first mosque built in India after the Islamic conquest, constructed from the demolished remains of 27 Hindu and Jain temples (the columns are reused, and the carved figures of Hindu deities are still visible, defaced but not entirely erased); the Alai Darwaza, a domed gateway built in 1311 by Alauddin Khalji that is considered one of the finest examples of the earliest Indo-Islamic architecture; the Tomb of Iltutmish; and the Iron Pillar — a 7-metre wrought-iron pillar from the Gupta period (4th–5th century CE) that has stood here for over 1,600 years without rusting, a metallurgical achievement that modern scientists still cannot fully explain. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1993.
history:
The Qutb Minar takes its name from Qutb ud-Din Aibak, the first Sultan of Delhi, who began its construction in 1193 following his master Muhammad of Ghor's defeat of the Rajput confederacy at the Second Battle of Tarain. The complex was built on the ruins of Lal Kot and Qila Rai Pithora, the fortified city of the Rajput king Prithviraj Chauhan — the last Hindu ruler of Delhi before the Islamic conquest.

The minaret was intended to serve as the tower for the call to prayer (azan) for the adjacent mosque, but its scale — far exceeding anything required for acoustics — suggests it was also a deliberate political statement: a tower of Islamic power visible across the plains of northern India. The first two storeys were built by Qutb ud-Din Aibak before his death in 1210; the next two were added by Iltutmish (his son-in-law and successor); the fifth storey was replaced by Firoz Shah Tughlaq in 1368 after lightning damage.

The Iron Pillar within the complex is older than everything around it by a thousand years. It was made in the Gupta period, sometime in the 4th or 5th century CE, and was originally erected in honour of the Vishnu deity. The pillar's extraordinary resistance to rust has been studied extensively: the current scientific consensus attributes it to high phosphorus content in the iron and a protective surface layer of iron hydrogen phosphate that prevents further oxidation.

timeline.event: Qutb ud-Din Aibak begins construction of the minaret and the Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque
Aibak dies; construction continues under Iltutmish
Iltutmish completes the minaret and adds his own tomb to the complex
Alauddin Khalji builds the Alai Darwaza gateway
Firoz Shah Tughlaq repairs the minaret after lightning damage; adds the current 5th storey
UNESCO designates Qutb Minar and its monuments a World Heritage Site

address: Mehrauli, New Delhi, Delhi 110030

entryFee.note: Includes the entire complex — Qutb Minar, Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque, Alai Darwaza, Iron Pillar, Iltutmish's Tomb, Alai Minar. Closed Mondays.

openingHours.note: Last entry 30 minutes before closing. Golden hour photography best 4–5pm
Closed

name: Jama Masjid
tagline: The largest mosque in India — Shah Jahan's final architectural statement, still drawing 25,000 worshippers every Friday.
description: The Jama Masjid of Delhi is the largest mosque in India and one of the grandest in the Islamic world — a building of such scale that its central courtyard can hold 25,000 worshippers simultaneously. Built by Emperor Shah Jahan between 1644 and 1656, it was his last major architectural commission before his son Aurangzeb imprisoned him in the Agra Fort, and he lavished on it resources that make even the Taj Mahal's construction look modest: 5,000 craftsmen, twelve years, and an expenditure of one million rupees. The mosque stands on a raised natural rock platform — visible from the Red Fort across Chandni Chowk — with three imposing gateways, four corner towers, and two 40-metre minarets of striped red sandstone and white marble. The courtyard, paved in black-and-white marble in a geometric pattern, is enclosed by arcaded corridors. The main prayer hall has eleven arches, a central dome flanked by two smaller domes, all clad in alternating black-and-white marble stripes. The mosque houses several sacred relics of the Prophet Muhammad — a strand of his hair, his sandal, and a Quran written in his own hand — displayed in the northern inner chamber. The view from the southern minaret (accessible for a small fee) is one of the finest views of Old Delhi available anywhere: a panorama of rooftops, the Red Fort ramparts, and the medieval street grid of Shahjahanabad laid out below.
history:
Shah Jahan had built the Red Fort as his palace and Chandni Chowk as his commercial avenue; the Jama Masjid — literally 'Friday Mosque' — was the third component of his vision for Shahjahanabad, the great mosque that would serve the spiritual needs of his new capital. Construction began in 1644, two years before the Red Fort was completed, and took twelve years.

The mosque remains a living institution. The Friday prayer draws tens of thousands of worshippers and is one of the most powerful religious gatherings visible in any Indian city. Non-Muslim visitors are welcome outside prayer times.

timeline.event: Emperor Shah Jahan begins construction of the Jama Masjid as the Friday mosque of his new capital Shahjahanabad
Mosque completed; inaugurated by Imam Syed Abdul Ghafoor Shah Bukhari from Bukhara
British forces briefly convert the mosque to military use after suppressing the Uprising

address: Jama Masjid, Chandni Chowk, New Delhi, Delhi 110006

entryFee.note: Free entry. Camera/video fee ₹300 (charged at gate). Minaret climb ₹100 per person. Closed during prayer times (Azan). Dress code strictly enforced — abaya available for rent at gate for ₹50.

openingHours.note: First session
Second session. Closed during all five daily prayer times.

name: India Gate
tagline: Delhi's most visited monument — the war memorial at the end of Kartavya Path where 84,000 names are written in stone.
description: India Gate is both Delhi's most visited monument and its most emotionally charged public space. The 42-metre triumphal arch, designed by Edwin Lutyens and completed in 1931, stands at the eastern end of Kartavya Path (formerly Rajpath) — the ceremonial axis of Lutyens' New Delhi — and bears the names of 84,000 Indian and British soldiers who died in the First World War and the Third Anglo-Afghan War. It is modelled on the Arc de Triomphe in Paris and shares with it the quality of monumental scale in a perfectly proportioned urban setting. The National War Memorial, a newer complex of black granite walls bearing the names of soldiers fallen since independence, has been built in the gardens immediately behind India Gate. The surrounding Kartavya Path lawn is Delhi's most important civic public space — the site of the Republic Day parade on January 26 every year, evening picnics for Delhi families, and the focal point of the New Delhi ceremonial district. At sunset and after dark, illuminated against the sky at the end of the grand avenue, India Gate is one of the most photogenic sights in any Indian city.
history: The idea of a war memorial for Indian soldiers who died in the First World War was proposed in 1917, while the war was still ongoing. Edwin Lutyens drew from his experience designing the Thiepval Memorial in France and the Menin Gate in Belgium. The foundation stone was laid in 1921 by the Duke of Connaught, and the memorial was inaugurated ten years later in 1931 by the Viceroy Lord Irwin. The 84,000 names inscribed on the arch are organised by regiment, rank, and alphabetical order. The Amar Jawan Jyoti eternal flame, originally lit in 1972 below a black marble cenotaph in memory of the Unknown Soldier of the 1971 India-Pakistan War, was merged with the National War Memorial flame in 2022.

timeline.event: All India War Memorial Committee established during World War I
Foundation stone laid by Duke of Connaught
India Gate inaugurated by Viceroy Lord Irwin; 84,000 names inscribed
Amar Jawan Jyoti eternal flame lit by Prime Minister Indira Gandhi, in memory of 1971 war martyrs
Amar Jawan Jyoti merged with National War Memorial flame; Rajpath renamed Kartavya Path

address: Kartavya Path, India Gate, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

entryFee.note: Completely free. Open 24 hours. Best visited at sunset (6–7 PM) or after dark when illuminated. Republic Day parade route — restricted access January 25–26.

openingHours.note: Open 24 hours; illuminated daily from sunset to 9:30 PM

name: Chandni Chowk
tagline: Shah Jahan's silver market — 375 years old, still the most intense kilometre of commerce, food, and history in India.
description: Chandni Chowk is the commercial and spiritual heart of Old Delhi — a kilometre-long boulevard that Shah Jahan designed and built in the 1650s as the primary market street of his new capital Shahjahanabad, running from the Red Fort's Lahori Gate westward to the Fatehpuri Mosque. The name means 'Moonlit Square' or 'Silver Market' — the original street had a reflecting pool at its centre, and the silver workshops that lined it gave it its name. Today, Chandni Chowk is simultaneously a wholesale trade district (electronics, spices, fabrics, wedding goods, stationery, hardware), a street food destination of national fame, and a pilgrimage route connecting some of Delhi's most important religious sites. Within 500 metres of each other stand the Jama Masjid (India's largest mosque), the Sis Ganj Sahib Gurudwara (one of Sikhism's most important historical sites, marking where Guru Tegh Bahadur was executed in 1675), the Gauri Shankar Temple (a 800-year-old Shiva temple), the Jain Lal Mandir (the oldest Jain temple in Delhi), and the Dharampura Haveli (a 19th-century merchant mansion that is now a heritage hotel). The food of Chandni Chowk deserves its own study: Parathewali Gali (the alley of stuffed bread), Natraj Dahi Bhalle (71 years old), Al Jawahar (Mughlai since 1947), the haleem at Qureshi's, the chole bhature at Sita Ram Diwan Chand, the jalebis at Old Famous Jalebi Wala — each a piece of edible history. The 2020 pedestrianisation of the main boulevard has significantly improved the walking experience.
history: Shah Jahan designed Chandni Chowk as the commercial centrepiece of Shahjahanabad from 1638 onwards. The street's original design included a canal running down its centre, fed from the Yamuna River. The British administration removed the canal in the late 19th century. The Chandni Chowk Redevelopment Project, completed in 2020, converted the main boulevard into a pedestrianised street.

timeline.event: Shah Jahan designs Chandni Chowk as the primary market street of Shahjahanabad; canal and central pool constructed
Guru Tegh Bahadur, the 9th Sikh Guru, executed on Chandni Chowk by Aurangzeb; Sis Ganj Sahib Gurudwara later built to mark the spot
British forces conduct reprisals along Chandni Chowk after suppressing the uprising
British administration removes the canal; street converted to a paved road
Chandni Chowk Redevelopment Project completed; main boulevard pedestrianised

address: Chandni Chowk, Old Delhi, Delhi 110006

entryFee.note: Free. Open daily. Metro: Chandni Chowk station (Yellow Line) exits directly onto the street.

openingHours.note: Most shops open by 10am; food stalls from 8am. Mornings less crowded.
Some wholesale shops closed Sunday; street food always open

name: Lodhi Garden
tagline: 90 acres of Mughal tombs inside a city park — where Delhi's power joggers share their morning with 500-year-old architecture.
description: Lodhi Garden is one of the most unusual public spaces in any capital city on earth: a 90-acre landscaped park in the middle of upscale South Delhi in which 15th and 16th-century Mughal monuments — real, UNESCO-protected historical architecture — are scattered among lawns, fountains, walking paths, and hedged gardens. The monuments include the Tomb of Muhammad Shah (the Sayyid ruler who died in 1444), the Tomb of Sikandar Lodi (died 1517), the Bara Gumbad (Large Dome, 1490s) with an adjacent mosque, and the Shish Gumbad (Glazed Dome, 1489) — all built in the transition period between the Sultanate and Mughal empires and representing the most complete surviving examples of Sayyid and Lodi architecture in India. The park was created by the British in 1936, who landscaped the grounds around the existing tombs and opened it as Lady Willingdon Park. It was renamed Lodhi Garden after independence. Today it serves simultaneously as Delhi's finest public garden, a morning exercise circuit for the Lutyens zone's powerful residents, a lunchtime escape for Khan Market office workers, and a photography destination for anyone who appreciates the improbable pleasure of a Mughal mausoleum next to a duck pond.
history: The monuments in Lodhi Garden were built during the Sayyid dynasty (1414–1451) and Lodi dynasty (1451–1526) — the last two dynasties of the Delhi Sultanate before the Mughal conquest. The British landscape architect created the current park layout in 1936. The park's residential location — surrounded by the most expensive properties in Delhi — has given it a distinctive character: exceptionally well-maintained, safe, and used by a mix of political and intellectual Delhi.

timeline.event: Tomb of Muhammad Shah (Sayyid) constructed
Shish Gumbad (Glazed Dome) built
Bara Gumbad and adjacent mosque constructed
Tomb of Sikandar Lodi constructed after his death
British landscape the area as Lady Willingdon Park, creating the current garden around existing monuments
Renamed Lodhi Garden after Indian independence

address: Lodhi Road, New Delhi, Delhi 110003

entryFee.note: Completely free. Open sunrise to sunset. Well lit; safe even for early morning walks.

openingHours.note: October–March: closes at 7:30pm. April–September: closes at 8pm.

name: Hazrat Nizamuddin Dargah
tagline: The Sufi shrine where qawwali fills the air every Thursday night — Delhi's most spiritually intense experience.
description: The Dargah (shrine) of Hazrat Nizamuddin Auliya is one of the most important Sufi shrines in South Asia and the most spiritually atmospheric place in Delhi. Nizamuddin Auliya (1238–1325) was the most revered saint of the Chishti order of Sufism — a mystic and poet who attracted followers across religious boundaries, preaching a philosophy of love and compassion that transcended the sectarianism of 13th-century Delhi. He is said to have had over 700 spiritual disciples, including Amir Khusrau, the great poet who is buried just outside his master's tomb and whose qawwalis (devotional music) are still sung at the shrine 700 years later. The dargah complex is a living, breathing religious institution: the marble shrine of Nizamuddin surrounded by the graves of those who wished to be buried near him — including Amir Khusrau, Jahanara Begum (Shah Jahan's daughter), and the poet Muhammad Iqbal — all enclosed within a courtyard that fills with devotees, supplicants, pilgrims, and musicians every day of the year. On Thursday evenings, when qawwali performances begin after the Maghrib prayer, the experience is unlike anything else available in any Indian city: the music, the incense, the candlelight, the packed courtyard of worshippers swaying in devotion, and the voices of the qawwali singers creating an atmosphere that has been continuous for seven centuries.
history: Nizamuddin Auliya was born in Budaun in 1238 and came to Delhi as a young man to study under the Sufi master Farid ud-Din Ganjshakar (Baba Farid). He established his own khanqah (hospice) in the village of Ghiyaspur, south of the walled city. He died in 1325, having lived through seven successive Delhi Sultans. Amir Khusrau, his closest disciple and the greatest poet of medieval India, reportedly died of grief six months after his master, and was buried just outside the dargah. The dargah survived partition in 1947 in part because of the universal reverence in which Nizamuddin is held across religious communities.

timeline.event: Nizamuddin Auliya born in Budaun
Establishes his khanqah in Ghiyaspur (present-day Nizamuddin)
Nizamuddin Auliya dies; buried in the present dargah
Amir Khusrau dies six months after his master; buried outside the dargah
Jahanara Begum (Shah Jahan's daughter) buried in the complex
Dargah survives partition violence due to its universal veneration across communities

address: Nizamuddin Basti, New Delhi, Delhi 110013

entryFee.note: Free entry. Head covering required (available at gate). Qawwali every Thursday after Maghrib prayer (~7 PM in winter, ~8 PM in summer). Remove footwear before entering inner shrine.

openingHours.note: Inner shrine accessible all day. Thursday qawwali is the unmissable visit.

name: Akshardham Temple Delhi
tagline: The world's largest Hindu temple complex — 20,000 carved figures, 1.1 billion carved pieces of pink sandstone, and no steel or concrete.
description: Akshardham is the largest Hindu temple complex in the world — a staggering feat of contemporary stone craftsmanship built between 2000 and 2005 by over 11,000 artisans and volunteers of the BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha. The central monument, dedicated to Bhagwan Swaminarayan, stands 43 metres tall, 96 metres wide, and 109 metres long, and is constructed entirely from Rajasthani pink sandstone and Italian marble — no steel or concrete anywhere in the structure, following the ancient Hindu Vastu and Pancharatra Shastra architectural principles. The exterior is carved with 20,000 individual figures of deities, saints, and animals across 234 decorated pillars; the main hall features intricate marble ceilings whose designs were extrapolated from manuscript illuminations. The complex's Sahaj Anand Water Show, a multimedia fountain experience using fire and water, runs nightly. The Neelkanth Yatra boat ride takes visitors through a recreation of 10,000 years of Indian history. The gardens are extraordinary — the Bharat Upavan includes over 60 bronze statues of India's historical figures. Regardless of religious affiliation, Akshardham is one of the most astonishing collective human achievements in contemporary architecture and craftsmanship available to see anywhere in the world. Guinness World Records certified it as the world's largest comprehensive Hindu temple in 2007.
history: Akshardham Delhi was built by BAPS Swaminarayan Sanstha on a 100-acre site on the banks of the Yamuna River provided by the Delhi government. The project was initiated by Pramukh Swami Maharaj. The 20,000 stone figures were carved by hand by craftsmen trained in the medieval stone-carving tradition of Gujarat and Rajasthan. The temple was inaugurated in November 2005 by the then President of India APJ Abdul Kalam and has received over 70 million visitors in the years since.

timeline.event: Construction begins on the Akshardham complex on a 100-acre Yamuna River site
Akshardham inaugurated by President APJ Abdul Kalam in November
Guinness World Records certifies Akshardham as the world's largest comprehensive Hindu temple

address: Noida Link Road, New Delhi, Delhi 110092

entryFee.note: Temple entry free. Exhibitions (Neelkanth Yatra boat ride, Sahaj Anand show) ticketed separately: ₹170–250 per person. Cameras and phones NOT permitted inside — must be deposited at the entrance. Closed Mondays.

openingHours.note: Water show at 7:45 PM. Book exhibition tickets in advance online to avoid queues.
Closed

name: Purana Qila
tagline: The sixth city of Delhi — where Humayun fell, Sher Shah ruled, and 2,500 years of continuous settlement have left their marks.
description: Purana Qila — the Old Fort — is one of Delhi's most dramatically beautiful monuments and one of its most archaeologically significant. The massive walls of grey quartzite that rise from the banks of a lake near Pragati Maidan enclose the remains of Dinpanah, the sixth city of Delhi, built by Emperor Humayun in the 1530s and captured and completed by the Afghan sultan Sher Shah Suri — Humayun's great rival who drove him into 15-year exile. The two most important buildings within the walls are the Qila-i-Kuhna Mosque (1541), built by Sher Shah and considered one of the finest examples of Afghan architecture in India, and the Sher Mandal, a double-storeyed octagonal tower from which Emperor Humayun fell to his death in 1556 while rushing down the stairs to answer the call to prayer. Archaeological excavations beneath Purana Qila have revealed evidence of continuous settlement from the Mauryan period (3rd century BCE) through the medieval period — making this site one of the candidates for the ancient city of Indraprastha, the capital of the Pandavas in the Mahabharata. Boating on the picturesque lake surrounding the fort walls is a popular activity, especially at dusk.
history: Humayun began building Dinpanah — his new capital — here in 1533. Before he could complete it, Sher Shah Suri defeated him twice and he fled to Persia in 1540. Sher Shah completed the fortification and renamed the city Shergarh. The ASI excavations conducted between 1954 and 1988 found Painted Grey Ware pottery dating to 1000–500 BCE.

timeline.event: Emperor Humayun begins construction of Dinpanah on the site
Sher Shah Suri defeats Humayun; completes and renames the city Shergarh
Qila-i-Kuhna Mosque completed by Sher Shah
Humayun falls to his death from the Sher Mandal tower stairs
ASI excavations reveal 2,500 years of continuous settlement including Painted Grey Ware from c. 1000 BCE

address: Purana Qila Road, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

entryFee.note: Sound and Light Show held evenings (separate ticket ₹80/150). Boating on the adjacent lake ₹50–80 per person. Museum inside free with entry ticket.

openingHours.note: Sound and Light Show: 7:30 PM (Hindi) and 8:30 PM (English)
Closed

name: National Museum New Delhi
tagline: India's greatest museum — 5,000 years of civilisation from the Indus Valley to the Mughal miniature, in 200,000 objects.
description: The National Museum of India in New Delhi is the country's foremost museum — the institution that holds the most comprehensive and historically significant collection of Indian art and artefacts assembled in any single building. With over 200,000 objects spanning 5,000 years of Indian civilisation — from terracotta figurines of the Indus Valley Civilisation (2600–1900 BCE) to Mughal miniature paintings, Chola bronzes, Gandharan sculptures, Tibetan Buddhist thangkas, and decorative art from every period and tradition of the subcontinent — it is the starting point for any serious engagement with Indian history and culture. The Harappan Civilisation gallery is perhaps the most significant single collection in the building — the dancing girl of Mohenjo-daro, the priest-king, and the thousands of seals whose undeciphered script continues to fascinate linguists worldwide. The Mughal miniature collection is exceptional. The arms and armour gallery, covering Islamic and Rajput weapons, is one of the finest in the world. Note: The museum is in transition as part of the Central Vista redevelopment — check current opening status before visiting.
history: The National Museum was established following a temporary exhibition of Indian art held in London and then in New Delhi in 1948. The present building opened in 1960 on Janpath. The museum is currently in the process of being relocated as part of the Central Vista redevelopment project.

timeline.event: Temporary exhibition of Indian art in New Delhi demonstrates public demand for a national museum
Temporary National Museum opens in Rashtrapati Bhavan
Permanent National Museum building opens on Janpath
New National Museum announced as part of Central Vista redevelopment; relocation planned

address: Janpath, New Delhi, Delhi 110011

entryFee.note: Photography fee ₹300. Audio guide available. Closed Mondays and national holidays. Verify current status before visiting — relocation works may affect access.

openingHours.note: Last entry at 5:30 PM
Closed

name: Jantar Mantar
tagline: An 18th-century open-air observatory in the heart of Connaught Place — where giant masonry instruments once measured the heavens.
description: Jantar Mantar is one of Delhi's most intellectually rewarding and visually unusual attractions — an open-air astronomical observatory built in 1724 by Maharaja Jai Singh II of Jaipur comprising 13 large-scale masonry instruments designed for naked-eye astronomical observation. It was the first of five such observatories Jai Singh commissioned across India (the others in Jaipur, Ujjain, Varanasi, and Mathura), built to compile astronomical tables, correct the existing calendar, and accurately predict the movements of the sun, moon, and planets. The instruments — built entirely from brick, limestone, and rubble, then plastered — include the Samrat Yantra (a giant equinoctial sundial), the Jai Prakash Yantra (hemispherical bowls for solar measurement), the Ram Yantra (cylindrical structures for altitude measurements), and the Misra Yantra (a mixed instrument for determining noon in different cities simultaneously). Unlike the more spectacular Jaipur Jantar Mantar — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — the Delhi observatory is smaller and less complete, but its location directly beside Connaught Place makes it uniquely accessible and useful as a 45-minute detour from any central Delhi itinerary. The geometric red-terracotta structures rising from a green lawn in the middle of a modern commercial district create a genuinely arresting image.
history: Maharaja Jai Singh II of Jaipur was a passionate astronomer who found the existing astronomical tables — both Islamic and Hindu — to be inaccurate. He petitioned the Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah (who was then the nominal emperor, though Mughal power had diminished) to allow him to build observatories to correct this. Construction began around 1721–1724. The Delhi observatory was the first and the most strategically placed, near the imperial capital. By 1867 the instruments had deteriorated significantly, and the observatory eventually fell out of scientific use. The Government of India has undertaken several restorations to maintain the site as a protected monument.

timeline.event: Maharaja Jai Singh II of Jaipur completes the Jantar Mantar observatory in Delhi — the first of his five observatories
Observatory falls into disrepair as modern astronomical instruments replace masonry instruments
Jantar Mantar featured on the logo of the Delhi Asian Games
ASI restoration works undertaken; site maintained as a protected monument

address: Jantar Mantar Road, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

entryFee.note: Children under 15 free. Open daily (unlike most ASI monuments, does not close on Mondays). Photography permitted without fee.

openingHours.note: Open daily. Best visited on a clear sunny day to appreciate the sundial instruments.

name: Lotus Temple
tagline: The Bahá'í House of Worship — a giant concrete lotus open to all faiths, and the most visited building in the world.
description: The Lotus Temple is the Bahá'í House of Worship in New Delhi — and according to various independent tourism surveys, one of the most visited buildings on earth, receiving over 4 million visitors annually at its peak. Designed by Iranian-Canadian architect Fariborz Sahba and completed in 1986, the structure takes the form of a half-open lotus flower, constructed from 27 free-standing marble-clad petals of reinforced concrete arranged in clusters of three. The nine-sided building — all Bahá'í temples have nine sides — rises 34.27 metres and seats 1,300 worshippers in a central hall flooded with natural light from the petals above. The Bahá'í faith teaches the essential unity of all religions; the temple is open to people of any faith (or none), with no sermons, rituals, or clergy. Visitors may sit quietly for prayer, meditation, or reflection. The approach through formal gardens with nine reflecting pools — representing the lotus stem — is one of the finest architectural promenades in Delhi. The surrounding gardens are peaceful and beautifully maintained. The Lotus Temple has won numerous architectural awards and is one of the most photographed buildings in India.
history: The Bahá'í faith was founded in Persia in the 19th century and teaches the progressive revelation of God through a succession of prophets including Abraham, Moses, Jesus, Muhammad, and Bahá'u'lláh. There are seven other Bahá'í Houses of Worship around the world, one on each continent. The New Delhi temple was commissioned in the 1970s, designed by Fariborz Sahba, and built between 1980 and 1986 using Greek marble for the petals. It was inaugurated in December 1986 and has since become one of Delhi's most popular attractions for both domestic tourists and international visitors.

timeline.event: Design competition for the New Delhi Bahá'í House of Worship won by architect Fariborz Sahba
Construction begins on the lotus-shaped structure
Lotus Temple inaugurated in December; opens to the public
Temple reported to have received over 70 million visitors since opening — one of the most visited buildings in the world

address: Lotus Temple Road, Bahapur, Shambhu Dayal Bagh, Bahapur, New Delhi, Delhi 110019

entryFee.note: Completely free. Closed Mondays. Bags must be left at the cloakroom (free). Silence maintained inside the prayer hall.

openingHours.note: Winter hours
Summer hours
Closed

name: Agrasen Ki Baoli
tagline: A medieval stepwell hidden in Connaught Place — 103 steps descending into cool shadow, and one of Delhi's most photogenic secrets.
description: Agrasen Ki Baoli is one of the most remarkable hidden monuments in all of Delhi — a 60-metre-long, 15-metre-wide stepwell of 103 steps descending through successive levels of arched chambers into the earth, located just off Hailey Road between Connaught Place and Kasturba Gandhi Marg, accessible in 10 minutes on foot from the Barakhamba Metro station. The stepwell's exact date of construction is disputed: it is attributed to the legendary king Agrasen — an ancient Hindu king said to have founded the city of Agroha in Haryana — but its current structure dates from the Tughluq or Lodi period (14th–15th century CE), making it at least 600 years old. The baoli (stepwell) format was the medieval solution to groundwater access: a descending staircase that follows the water table, with chambers at each level providing shade, ventilation, and places for travellers to rest. Agrasen Ki Baoli is a particularly well-preserved example of the type, with three levels of arched galleries in a uniform Indo-Islamic style. Today it is protected by ASI, and its combination of architectural drama, historical mystery, and extraordinary photographic quality — especially in the early morning light — makes it one of Delhi's most rewarding and least-crowded attractions. It has also gained recent fame as a filming location for several Bollywood productions.
history: The exact historical record of Agrasen Ki Baoli is unclear. The structure is attributed by tradition to the legendary king Agrasen, who is venerated by the Aggarwal community as their founding patriarch, but there is no historical evidence for this attribution. The current structure's architectural style suggests construction during the Tughluq or Lodi sultanate periods — likely 14th or 15th century. The baoli was restored in the early 20th century with contributions from the Aggarwal community. It was subsequently protected by the Archaeological Survey of India.

timeline.event: Current stepwell structure built, likely during the Tughluq or Lodi sultanate period
Restored with contributions from the Aggarwal community
Protected ASI monument; popular photography destination

address: Hailey Road, Near Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

entryFee.note: Completely free. Open sunrise to sunset. Best visited early morning (7–9 AM) for good light and no crowds. 10 min walk from Barakhamba Road or Janpath Metro stations.

openingHours.note: Best visited in morning light. Not floodlit at night.

name: Raj Ghat
tagline: Mahatma Gandhi's memorial on the banks of the Yamuna — a simple black marble platform where India's greatest leader was cremated.
description: Raj Ghat is the memorial to Mahatma Gandhi — the site on the western bank of the Yamuna River where his body was cremated on January 31, 1948, the day after his assassination by Nathuram Godse. The memorial is deliberately simple: a black marble platform, slightly raised from the surrounding garden, with an eternal flame burning at one end and the words 'Hey Ram' — the last words Gandhi is said to have spoken — inscribed in his own handwriting in the stone. The surrounding gardens, designed by landscape architect Vanu G. Bapat, are immaculate: green lawns, flowering trees, and the quiet of the Yamuna floodplain just behind. The memorial is one of the most emotionally resonant sites in India — the place where the world came to pay its respects to Gandhi, where Nehru said 'the light has gone out of our lives,' and where visiting heads of state have laid flowers for seven decades. Adjacent to Raj Ghat is the Gandhi Smriti park complex, which also contains Shanti Vana (the memorial to Jawaharlal Nehru), Vijay Ghat (Lal Bahadur Shastri), and several other prime ministerial memorials, making this a significant cluster of 20th-century Indian political history.
history: Mahatma Gandhi was assassinated on January 30, 1948, at Birla House in New Delhi, while walking to an evening prayer meeting. He was shot three times by Nathuram Godse. His body was cremated the following day at Raj Ghat, on the banks of the Yamuna — a site chosen for its traditional sacred significance as a cremation ground beside a river. The funeral was attended by over one million people. The memorial was designed and laid out in 1948–1951 in its current form. Every year on Gandhi's birthday (October 2, declared International Day of Non-Violence by the UN) and his death anniversary (January 30), the Indian Prime Minister and President lay flowers here in an official ceremony.

timeline.event: Mahatma Gandhi assassinated on January 30; cremated at Raj Ghat on January 31
Memorial gardens designed and laid out
UN designates October 2 (Gandhi's birthday) as International Day of Non-Violence

address: Ring Road, Gandhi Smriti, New Delhi, Delhi 110006

entryFee.note: Completely free. Shoes must be removed before entering the memorial platform. Closed Mondays.

openingHours.note: October 2 and January 30: special ceremonies attended by national leadership
Closed

name: Hauz Khas Village
tagline: A 14th-century reservoir surrounded by contemporary Delhi — the city's most interesting collision of medieval ruins and modern restaurants.
description: Hauz Khas Village is the most architecturally and culturally layered neighbourhood in Delhi — a small enclave of lanes where 14th-century Sultanate monuments (a madrasa, a mosque, the tomb of Firuz Shah Tughlaq, and the vast Hauz Khas reservoir that gives the area its name) coexist with contemporary restaurants, boutique fashion shops, design studios, and some of the city's most serious independent galleries. The Hauz Khas reservoir itself was built by Alauddin Khalji in 1296 to supply water to his new city of Siri, and enlarged by Firuz Shah Tughlaq in the 14th century — the ruins of his madrasa and tomb still stand directly on the reservoir's edge, and the view of the water, the deer from the adjacent Deer Park, and the medieval stonework at dusk is one of the most genuinely beautiful views in any South Asian city. The village lanes have been occupied by restaurants and shops since the 1990s, making it one of Delhi's longest-established 'alternative' neighbourhoods. Today it houses some of the city's most interesting independent restaurants — Italian, Japanese, Mediterranean, Mexican, and inventive Indian — alongside vintage clothing, ceramics, and independent bookshops. The Hauz Khas Metro station (Yellow Line) puts central Delhi 15 minutes away.
history: The Hauz Khas ('Royal Tank') reservoir was originally constructed by Alauddin Khalji in 1296 as the water supply for his city of Siri. Firuz Shah Tughlaq (1351–1388) enlarged the tank, dredged it, and built on its banks a madrasa (Islamic seminary) and his own tomb — the complex visible today. After the decline of the Tughluq dynasty, the area was gradually absorbed into the urban fabric of Delhi and the madrasa fell out of use. The village around it developed organically over centuries and became Delhi's first 'bohemian' neighbourhood when artists and designers began moving into its cheap rental spaces in the late 1980s.

timeline.event: Alauddin Khalji constructs the Hauz Khas reservoir as the water supply for his city Siri
Firuz Shah Tughlaq enlarges the reservoir and builds his madrasa and tomb on its banks
Artists and designers begin moving into the village; emergence of the first boutique shops and galleries
Village establishes itself as Delhi's premier bohemian neighbourhood; restaurants proliferate

address: Hauz Khas Village, New Delhi, Delhi 110016

entryFee.note: The village and lanes are free. Monument entry (madrasa/tomb complex) is free. Deer Park adjacent is free.

openingHours.note: Shops and restaurants typically open 11am; best visited late afternoon through evening.

name: Dilli Haat
tagline: India's 29 states in one outdoor market — handicrafts, textiles, and regional food from every corner of the country, across from INA Metro.
description: Dilli Haat INA is the most intelligently designed crafts market in India — a permanent outdoor bazaar operated by the Delhi Tourism and Transportation Development Corporation that rotates its craft stalls among artisans from all 29 Indian states on a fortnightly basis, ensuring that the inventory constantly refreshes and that no single state dominates the market for long. The concept was to create a marketplace that gives direct access between travelling craftspeople — Kashmiri papier-mâché artists, Rajasthani block-printers, Madhubani painters from Bihar, Warli artists from Maharashtra, Manipuri weavers — and urban consumers, without the intermediary of a wholesaler. The result, open since 1994, has become one of Delhi's most reliable stops for anyone who wants to buy genuinely Indian handicrafts at fair prices. The food court along the southern edge of the market is organised by state — each stall represents a different regional cuisine — creating what is probably the most geographically diverse food court in the country: Kashmiri wazwan, Kerala fish curry, Assamese pork, Rajasthani dal baati churma, Bengali mishti doi, and Goan fish all available within 200 metres of each other. The market is directly across from INA Metro station (Pink Line) and adjacent to INA Market, Delhi's best wholesale spice and dry fruit bazaar.
history: Dilli Haat was conceived in the early 1990s by the Delhi Tourism and Transportation Development Corporation as a solution to the problem of craft tourism: how to give travellers genuine access to India's extraordinary diversity of handicrafts without requiring them to travel to 29 different states. The INA branch, the original and most central, opened in 1994. A second branch subsequently opened in Pitampura in North Delhi. The rotating residency model — artisans stay for 15 days and are then replaced by a new cohort — has kept the market fresh for three decades.

timeline.event: Dilli Haat INA opens; first permanent rotating craft market in India
Second Dilli Haat opens in Pitampura, North Delhi

address: Sri Aurobindo Marg, INA Colony, New Delhi, Delhi 110023

entryFee.note: Small entry fee. Open all days. Best time: weekday afternoons when less crowded. Artisans rotate every 15 days.

openingHours.note: Food stalls often open until 10pm. Closed only for a few national holidays.

name: Connaught Place
tagline: The imperial heart of Lutyens' Delhi — a Georgian colonnaded circle that remains the commercial and social centre of the capital.
description: Connaught Place — officially renamed Rajiv Chowk but universally known by its colonial name — is the commercial, social, and geographical centre of New Delhi. Designed by British architect Robert Tor Russell and built between 1929 and 1933 as the retail and business hub of Lutyens' planned capital, it consists of two concentric circles of Georgian-style colonnaded buildings — the Inner Circle and Outer Circle — arranged around a central park, with seven radial roads connecting it to the rest of the city. The architecture is distinctive and handsome: cream-painted colonnaded facades running in an unbroken arc, punctuated by the entrances to shops, restaurants, banks, and offices that have occupied the same spaces for nearly a century. Connaught Place (CP) today functions as Delhi's premier address for flagship stores, multinational offices, heritage restaurants (Wengers, since 1926; Embassy, since 1949; Kwality, since 1940), underground bars, and the city's densest cluster of Metro connectivity — Rajiv Chowk station, directly beneath Central Park, is the busiest interchange on the entire Delhi Metro network. The underground Palika Bazaar market beneath Central Park is one of Delhi's most chaotic and entertaining shopping experiences. CP also sits within 10 minutes' walking distance of Jantar Mantar, Agrasen Ki Baoli, the National Museum, and Janpath Market — making it the natural hub for a walking day in central Delhi.
history: Connaught Place was designed by Robert Tor Russell, Chief Architect of the Public Works Department, as part of Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker's master plan for New Delhi. Named after Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught (Queen Victoria's son), it was built between 1929 and 1933 and formally inaugurated along with New Delhi on February 13, 1931. The Georgian neoclassical design — colonnaded arcades on both floors — was intended to give the commercial district a consistency and dignity befitting an imperial capital. For decades after independence it remained Delhi's most prestigious address. The underground Palika Bazaar was added in 1978. The Metro station (opened 2005) cemented its centrality in the modern city. A major renovation in 2010 restored the facades and repaved the central park.

timeline.event: Construction of Connaught Place begins under Chief Architect Robert Tor Russell
New Delhi formally inaugurated on February 13; Connaught Place opens as the commercial hub
Underground Palika Bazaar constructed beneath Central Park
Officially renamed Rajiv Chowk; colloquial name 'Connaught Place' continues in universal use
Rajiv Chowk Metro station opens; becomes busiest interchange on the Delhi Metro
Major renovation restores facades and Central Park

address: Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

entryFee.note: Free. Open all days. Metro: Rajiv Chowk (Yellow + Blue Line interchange). Palika Bazaar underground market has a nominal entry.

openingHours.note: Shops typically open 10am–9pm. Restaurants and bars until 11pm. Central Park open 24 hours.

name: Majnu Ka Tila
tagline: Delhi's Tibetan village — momos, thangkas, butter tea, and Buddhist monasteries on the banks of the Yamuna.
description: Majnu Ka Tila is Delhi's Tibetan refugee colony — a dense, self-contained neighbourhood of narrow lanes on the western bank of the Yamuna River in North Delhi that has functioned as the city's Tibetan cultural enclave since the early 1960s, when Tibetan refugees began settling here following the Chinese annexation of Tibet and the Dalai Lama's flight to India in 1959. Today it is one of Delhi's most distinctive and rewarding neighbourhood experiences: a compact grid of lanes packed with Tibetan restaurants serving some of the best momos (dumplings), thukpa (noodle soup), butter tea, and tsampa (roasted barley) available anywhere outside Tibet or Dharamsala; shops selling thangka paintings, prayer wheels, singing bowls, Buddhist texts, and Tibetan handicrafts; and the characteristic sounds and smells of a community that has maintained its culture with extraordinary tenacity across six decades of exile. Several Buddhist monasteries and temples are located within the colony, and the atmosphere on religious days — particularly Losar (Tibetan New Year) — is unlike anything else available in Delhi. The neighbourhood is officially called Aruna Nagar but universally known as Majnu Ka Tila (a name derived from a Sufi saint who once meditated here). It is accessible from Vidhan Sabha Metro station (Red Line) or by autorickshaw from Civil Lines.
history: The Tibetan community began settling in this area in the early 1960s following Tibet's incorporation into China and the subsequent flight of the Dalai Lama to Dharamsala. The Delhi government allocated land here to Tibetan refugees, who established a self-governing community. The colony has grown over six decades into a fully functioning Tibetan town-in-miniature, with its own schools, monasteries, shops, and social institutions. It is formally administered under the Central Tibetan Administration.

timeline.event: Dalai Lama flees Tibet; tens of thousands of Tibetan refugees begin arriving in India
Delhi government allocates land at Majnu Ka Tila to Tibetan refugees; colony established
Colony grows; monasteries, schools, and commercial lanes develop
One of India's most vibrant Tibetan communities; a major cultural and culinary destination

address: Aruna Nagar, Majnu Ka Tila, New Delhi, Delhi 110054

entryFee.note: Free. Metro: Vidhan Sabha (Red Line), then 10-min autorickshaw. Best visited on weekends when the colony is most lively. Losar (Tibetan New Year, Feb/Mar) is the best time to visit.

openingHours.note: Restaurants open from 8am; shops from 10am. Monasteries open early morning.

name: Waste to Wonder Park
tagline: Seven Wonders of the World, built entirely from scrap metal — the most inventive public park in Delhi.
description: Waste to Wonder Park is one of Delhi's most creative and unusual public spaces — a 7.5-acre park on the banks of the Yamuna near Sarai Kale Khan in which full-scale replicas of the Seven Wonders of the World have been constructed entirely from recycled industrial waste and scrap metal: the Eiffel Tower, the Taj Mahal, the Statue of Liberty, the Colosseum, the Leaning Tower of Pisa, the Great Wall of China, and the Sydney Opera House. The park was developed by the South Delhi Municipal Corporation and opened in 2019 as an initiative to demonstrate the creative potential of waste recycling while giving Delhi families a photogenic and educational public space. The sculptures are genuinely impressive — the Eiffel Tower replica stands 60 feet tall and was assembled from discarded steel pipes, door handles, cycle parts, and industrial scrap — and the park is beautifully lit at night, making it one of Delhi's most photogenic after-dark destinations. It won the United Nations Environment Programme's Asia Environment Enforcement Award in 2019. There is also a science park section and food court within the grounds. The park is particularly popular with families and school groups and is best visited in the evening when the illuminations come on.
history: The park was conceptualised by the South Delhi Municipal Corporation as an initiative combining environmental messaging with public recreation. The construction used over 150 tonnes of scrap material collected from across the city. It opened to the public in February 2019 and quickly became one of South Delhi's most visited parks, attracting over a million visitors in its first year.

timeline.event: Construction begins using 150+ tonnes of recycled scrap metal
Waste to Wonder Park opens in February; receives UN Environment Programme Asia award
Becomes one of Delhi's most visited family parks; over 1 million visitors in first year

address: Sarai Kale Khan, Near Rajiv Gandhi Smriti Van, New Delhi, Delhi 110013

entryFee.note: Entry ₹50 per adult, ₹25 per child. Open all days. Best visited after 6 PM when the illuminated sculptures are at their most impressive.

openingHours.note: Illuminations begin at sunset (~6 PM). Evening visits strongly recommended.

name: Gandhi Smriti
tagline: The house where Gandhi spent his last 144 days — and the exact spot where he was assassinated on January 30, 1948.
description: Gandhi Smriti is the most personally resonant Gandhi memorial in India — not an abstract monument like Raj Ghat, but the actual house where Mahatma Gandhi lived, worked, prayed, and was killed. The building, formerly called Birla House, is a late 19th-century mansion in the Lutyens bungalow zone on Tees January Marg, where Gandhi stayed during his final visit to Delhi from September 1947 until his assassination on January 30, 1948 — 144 days in total. The museum preserves his room exactly as it was: the simple charpoy (rope bed), the spinning wheel, his sandals, his spectacles, the few possessions of a man who owned almost nothing. The garden at the rear of the house marks the prayer ground where Gandhi held his evening prayer meetings, and a glass-enclosed 'Martyr's Column' stands on the precise spot — measured to the centimetre — where he fell after being shot three times by Nathuram Godse at 5:17 PM. A line of footprints in the pathway marks Gandhi's last walk from the house to the prayer ground. The permanent exhibition — 'Eternal Gandhi' — uses dioramas, photographs, manuscripts, and multimedia to trace his life from Porbandar to Pretoria to Dandi to Delhi. The museum is intimate, uncrowded, and extraordinarily moving in a way that large memorial sites rarely achieve.
history: Birla House was the Delhi residence of the industrialist G.D. Birla, who offered it to Gandhi whenever he visited Delhi. Gandhi was deeply reluctant to stay in such a large house but was persuaded on practical grounds — the house had grounds large enough for his evening prayer meetings, which regularly drew thousands of people. He arrived in September 1947 in the aftermath of Partition, to try to calm the communal violence that had engulfed Delhi. He fasted several times during this period. On January 20, 1948, a bomb exploded at his prayer meeting — the first assassination attempt. Ten days later, on January 30, Nathuram Godse shot him three times as he walked to the prayer ground. He died within minutes. The Government of India acquired Birla House in 1971 and converted it into the Gandhi Smriti museum, which opened in 1973.

timeline.event: Gandhi arrives at Birla House in September; begins his final stay in Delhi
First assassination attempt on January 20; Gandhi assassinated on January 30 at 5:17 PM
Government of India acquires Birla House from the Birla family
Gandhi Smriti museum opens to the public

address: 5, Tees January Marg, New Delhi, Delhi 110011

entryFee.note: Completely free. Closed Mondays and national holidays. Photography permitted in the garden and exterior; restricted inside the house.

openingHours.note: Last entry at 5 PM. Guided tours available without prior booking.

name: National Gallery of Modern Art
tagline: India's foremost modern art museum — 17,000 works from 1850 to the present, in a converted colonial palace.
description: The National Gallery of Modern Art (NGMA) in New Delhi is the premier institution for modern and contemporary Indian art — a collection of over 17,000 works spanning from 1850 to the present day, housed in a building that is itself architecturally significant: the former Jaipur House, the Delhi residence of the Maharaja of Jaipur, built in the 1930s in a restrained colonial neoclassical style. The collection documents the full arc of modern Indian art, from the Bengal School's rediscovery of indigenous artistic traditions at the turn of the 20th century — represented by works of Abanindranath Tagore, Nandalal Bose, and Rabindranath Tagore — through the Progressive Artists' Group's engagement with European modernism in the 1940s and 50s (F.N. Souza, S.H. Raza, M.F. Husain), to post-independence abstraction, the Baroda School, and contemporary practice. The sculpture collection, displayed through the building's light-filled galleries and the outdoor lawn, is among the strongest in India. Rotating temporary exhibitions — often the first major Indian showing of significant international artists — make return visits rewarding. The NGMA sits directly at the India Gate end of Kartavya Path, making it a natural addition to any India Gate visit.
history: The NGMA was established in 1954 by the Government of India, with the Jaipur House building repurposed as its home. The original collection was seeded with works acquired from the 1953 Academy of Arts exhibition. An extension wing was added in 2009, significantly expanding the display and storage space. The New Delhi branch is the main institution; sister branches exist in Mumbai (at the Sir Cowasji Jehangir Public Hall) and Bangalore.

timeline.event: Jaipur House built as the Delhi residence of the Maharaja of Jaipur

address: Jaipur House, India Gate, New Delhi, Delhi 110003

entryFee.note: Photography permitted without flash. Audio guide available. Closed Mondays and national holidays.

openingHours.note: Last entry at 6 PM. Check website for current temporary exhibitions before visiting.

name: Gurudwara Bangla Sahib
tagline: Delhi's most prominent Sikh gurdwara — the golden dome beside Connaught Place where langar has been served free to all for 350 years.
description: Gurudwara Bangla Sahib is the most important and most visited Sikh gurdwara in Delhi — a magnificent complex of white marble and gilded domes beside Connaught Place whose sacred pool (sarovar), golden sanctum, and round-the-clock langar (community kitchen) make it one of the most welcoming religious sites in any Indian city. The gurdwara is built on the site of a bungalow where Guru Har Krishan, the eighth Sikh Guru, stayed during his visit to Delhi in 1664. The young Guru — he was just eight years old — devoted himself to serving the victims of a smallpox and cholera epidemic that was devastating the city, and is said to have used the water of the well on the site to heal the sick. He contracted the illness himself and died in Delhi shortly after. The sarovar (sacred pool) that now occupies the site of that well is believed to have healing properties, and devotees come from across India and the world to bathe in or collect its water. The gurdwara runs one of Delhi's largest langars — the Sikh tradition of free communal meals regardless of religion, caste, or status — serving tens of thousands of meals daily. Visitors of all faiths are not just permitted but actively welcomed to eat. The complex is operational 24 hours a day. The golden dome, illuminated at night, is one of the most recognisable landmarks of central Delhi.
history: The bungalow on this site belonged to Raja Jai Singh II of Amber (the same Maharaja who built Jantar Mantar). Guru Har Krishan, the eighth Sikh Guru, stayed here during his visit to Delhi in 1664 at the invitation of Emperor Aurangzeb. During the epidemic, he is said to have distributed the water from the well to the sick without distinction of religion or caste. After his death in Delhi at age eight, the site became sacred to Sikhs. The gurdwara was formally established in the 18th century and the current structure — the large marble complex with the golden dome and sarovar — was built in the 20th century. It is administered by the Shiromani Gurdwara Parbandhak Committee (SGPC).

timeline.event: Guru Har Krishan, 8th Sikh Guru, stays at the bungalow on this site; serves victims of Delhi's epidemic
Guru Har Krishan contracts smallpox and dies in Delhi at age eight; site becomes sacred

address: Ashoka Road, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

entryFee.note: Completely free, including langar. Head must be covered — scarves available free at entrance. Remove footwear before entering. Open 24 hours. Langar served around the clock.

openingHours.note: Open 24 hours. Golden dome illuminated from dusk. Langar available at all hours.
